content.text: Once token costs are tagged and attributed to teams and features, the organisation must decide what to do with that information. The two dominant strategies are showback and chargeback. Showback makes cost visible without any financial consequence; chargeback transfers actual costs to the consuming team's budget. Neither is universally better — the right choice depends on organisational maturity, the culture around financial accountability, and the agent's role in the P&L.

attrs.alt: Showback vs Chargeback decision flow: both branch from tagged usage ledger, showback produces visibility reports, chargeback produces financial transfers

content.text: Showback: visibility without financial consequence
A showback report shows teams what they would have been charged if chargeback were in effect, without actually moving money. This approach is appropriate when:
AI adoption is in an early or experimental phase and teams are still learning how tokens translate to cost.
The tagging infrastructure is newly deployed and has not yet achieved enough coverage to support accurate chargeback.
Shared agents serve multiple teams in proportions that change rapidly, making precise attribution impractical until usage patterns stabilise.
Cost is classified as a central AI platform investment rather than a product line cost-of-goods, so teams are not expected to bear it directly.

Showback builds the organisational readiness for chargeback. Teams become familiar with the cost structure, product managers start asking about cost per feature, and the FinOps function learns which numbers to trust before they have financial consequences.
Chargeback: financial accountability

In a chargeback model, the AI platform team acts as an internal service provider. At each billing period end, it issues an internal invoice to each consuming cost centre. That invoice reduces the cost centre's available budget. Chargeback creates direct financial incentives for efficiency that showback alone cannot:
Teams with budget pressure will actively optimise prompt length, choose cheaper model tiers where quality permits, and suppress redundant agent calls that do not generate value.
Product P&Ls accurately reflect the true cost of AI features, enabling real margin analysis.
The AI platform team can recover its costs rather than absorbing them on behalf of the whole organisation.

Chargeback requires: at least 95% tag coverage (unallocated costs must not be quietly attributed to any team), a clear dispute resolution process, and a billing cadence that teams can plan around (monthly is standard).
Implementing a chargeback period-end process

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from decimal import Decimal
from datetime import date
from typing import Dict, List


@dataclass
class UsageRecord:
    cost_centre: str
    team: str
    feature: str
    cost_usd: Decimal
    period_start: date
    period_end: date


@dataclass
class ChargebackInvoice:
    cost_centre: str
    period_start: date
    period_end: date
    line_items: List[Dict[str, object]] = field(default_factory=list)
    total_usd: Decimal = Decimal("0")

    def add_line(self, team: str, feature: str, cost: Decimal) -> None:
        self.line_items.append({"team": team, "feature": feature, "cost_usd": float(cost)})
        self.total_usd += cost

    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "cost_centre": self.cost_centre,
            "period": f"{self.period_start} to {self.period_end}",
            "line_items": self.line_items,
            "total_usd": float(self.total_usd),
        }


def generate_chargeback_invoices(
    records: List[UsageRecord],
) -> Dict[str, ChargebackInvoice]:
    """
    Aggregate usage records by cost centre and generate one invoice per centre.
    """
    invoices: Dict[str, ChargebackInvoice] = {}
    for r in records:
        if r.cost_centre not in invoices:
            invoices[r.cost_centre] = ChargebackInvoice(
                cost_centre=r.cost_centre,
                period_start=r.period_start,
                period_end=r.period_end,
            )
        invoices[r.cost_centre].add_line(r.team, r.feature, r.cost_usd)
    return invoices


# --- Example ---
records = [
    UsageRecord("CC-4401", "payments", "fraud-check",   Decimal("1240.50"), date(2026,6,1), date(2026,6,30)),
    UsageRecord("CC-4401", "payments", "kyc-verify",    Decimal("380.20"),  date(2026,6,1), date(2026,6,30)),
    UsageRecord("CC-2200", "risk-eng", "limit-review",  Decimal("550.00"),  date(2026,6,1), date(2026,6,30)),
]
invoices = generate_chargeback_invoices(records)
for inv in invoices.values():
    print(inv.to_dict())
# CC-4401: total $1,620.70  (2 line items)
# CC-2200: total $550.00    (1 line item)
Choosing between showback and chargeback
Criterion
Showback
Chargeback
Tagging coverage required
> 70% (errors are informational)
> 95% (errors cause disputes)
ERP integration needed
No
Yes — GL journal entries
Dispute process required
Informal
Formal SLA with Finance
Behavioural incentive
Weak — no budget impact
Strong — hits team budget
Implementation complexity
Low
Medium-high
Best for
Pilots, R&D, platform agents
Production cost-of-goods agents
The hybrid approach
Most enterprises run both models simultaneously, applied at the agent class level:
Experimental and R&D agents
→ showback only. Teams see their spend without budget impact, which avoids penalising exploration.
Production agents generating revenue or serving customers
→ full chargeback. The cost is a genuine COGS item and should be reflected in the product P&L.
Shared platform agents (model routing, embedding generation, guardrails)

→ showback with proportional split. Full chargeback is impractical because these costs cannot be precisely attributed; showback keeps them visible while proportional split creates a reasonable approximation.
Dispute resolution for chargeback
When chargeback is in effect, teams will raise disputes. The most common dispute triggers:
Incorrect tag assignment — the wrong team tag was used in a rush deployment. The team sees charges for calls they did not make.
Unexpected cost spikes — a batch job ran longer than anticipated or a new model tier was silently selected by a routing layer.
Shared-agent allocation disputes — a team believes the proportional split overrepresents their usage.

A formal dispute SLA should include: a 10 business-day window to raise a dispute after receiving the invoice, raw usage log access for the team's tagged calls so they can independently verify, a Finance/FinOps arbitration step with a 5 business-day resolution target, and a credit memo process to correct verified errors in the next billing cycle.
Showback report format
A showback report should present cost information in a way that team leads and product managers can act on:

# showback-report-2026-06.yaml
period: 2026-06
generated_at: "2026-07-03T09:00:00Z"

cost_centres:
  CC-4401:
    team: payments
    total_cost_usd: 1620.70
    budget_usd: 2000.00
    utilisation_pct: 81.0
    breakdown_by_feature:
      fraud-check:  1240.50
      kyc-verify:    380.20
    breakdown_by_model:
      claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022: 1480.00
      claude-3-5-haiku-20241022:   140.70
    trend:
      may_2026: 1380.00
      apr_2026: 1210.00
      mom_change_pct: 17.4
    notes: "Spike driven by fraud-check batch job 2026-06-18 (3.2M tokens)."

  CC-2200:
    team: risk-eng
    total_cost_usd: 550.00
    budget_usd: 800.00
    utilisation_pct: 68.8
    breakdown_by_feature:
      limit-review: 550.00
    trend:
      may_2026: 520.00
      apr_2026: 490.00
      mom_change_pct: 5.8
Transition plan: showback to chargeback

Moving from showback to chargeback is a governance change that requires preparation across FinOps, Finance, Engineering, and team leads. A structured transition reduces disputes and maintains team trust.
Establish baseline visibility (months 1–2)
— run showback for at least two full billing cycles. Share reports with team leads monthly. Identify any teams whose spend is materially above their expected share.
Reach tagging coverage threshold (month 2–3)
— track the unallocated percentage in the usage ledger. Block new production deployments that do not include complete allocation tags. Target <5% unallocated before proceeding.
Load AI budgets in Finance systems (month 3)
— work with Finance to add an 'AI API' line to each cost centre's opex budget. Use the showback baseline to propose initial budget levels. Get approval from department heads before chargeback begins.
Pilot chargeback with one team (month 4)
— run a full billing cycle for a single high-volume team that has high tagging coverage. Work through any dispute scenarios before broader rollout.
Full rollout (months 5–6)
— extend chargeback to all production agents. Continue showback for experimental agents. Publish dispute SLA and provide all teams with access to their own usage data.
Incentive effects and unintended consequences
Chargeback creates powerful incentives that must be monitored to prevent gaming and value-destroying optimisation.
Prompt truncation for cost reduction
— teams may reduce system prompt length or context windows to cut input token costs, at the expense of agent quality. Monitor task success rates alongside cost metrics.
Avoiding high-value but expensive use cases

— a team whose budget is under pressure may deprioritise AI features that have high ROI but visible token cost. FinOps must ensure that cost reports are always accompanied by value metrics, not presented in isolation.
Tag manipulation
— teams may route calls through another team's tag key to shift costs. Tag governance controls (registry validation, anomaly detection on tag volume shifts) prevent this.
Delayed deployment for budget cycle timing
— teams with low remaining budget may defer production launches to the next fiscal period. A rolling twelve-month budget view reduces this behaviour.
Reporting cadence and audience
Showback and chargeback data serves different audiences at different frequencies:
Audience
Cadence
Key metrics
Team leads / engineers
Real-time dashboard
Token/day by feature, cost vs. budget %
Product managers
Weekly digest
Cost per feature, MoM trend, budget burn rate
Department heads / CFO
Monthly chargeback invoice
Total AI spend, forecast vs. budget, variance
AI Platform team
Monthly recovery report
Revenue from chargeback, unallocated pool size
Adjustments, credits, and reconciliation
Chargeback numbers are rarely perfect on first issue. A reconciliation workflow handles the gap between the invoiced amount and the correct amount:
Provider credits

— if the model provider issues a credit for a service disruption, that credit must be passed through to teams proportionally. The simplest approach is to apply the credit as a percentage reduction to all invoices in the affected period.
Tag corrections

— when a tag error is confirmed in a dispute, the FinOps system issues a credit memo to the incorrectly charged cost centre and a debit memo to the correct one. Both are reflected in the following billing cycle.
Commitment discount pass-through

— if the AI platform team has negotiated volume commitments with the provider that reduce per-token rates, the internal rate card used for chargeback should reflect the discounted rate, not the public list price. The discount benefit flows to consuming teams rather than being captured by the platform team.

Reconciliation is easier when chargeback invoices include a reference to the raw usage log segment that generated each line item. Teams can independently verify the numbers without needing to request data from FinOps.
Practical example: monthly chargeback run

import json
from decimal import Decimal
from datetime import date
from typing import List, Dict

# Simplified end-of-month chargeback run
# Assumes usage_records is a list of dicts loaded from the usage ledger

def run_monthly_chargeback(
    usage_records: List[Dict],
    internal_rates: Dict[str, Dict[str, Decimal]],
    period_end: date,
    unallocated_threshold: float = 0.05,
) -> Dict:
    total_cost = Decimal("0")
    unallocated = Decimal("0")
    invoices: Dict[str, Decimal] = {}

    for record in usage_records:
        model = record["model"]
        rates = internal_rates.get(model, {})
        if not rates:
            continue
        cost = (
            Decimal(record["input_tokens"]) / Decimal("1000000") * rates["input"]
            + Decimal(record["output_tokens"]) / Decimal("1000000") * rates["output"]
        )
        total_cost += cost
        cc = record.get("tags", {}).get("cost_centre")
        if cc:
            invoices[cc] = invoices.get(cc, Decimal("0")) + cost
        else:
            unallocated += cost

    unallocated_ratio = float(unallocated / total_cost) if total_cost else 0
    if unallocated_ratio > unallocated_threshold:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Unallocated spend {unallocated_ratio:.1%} exceeds threshold {unallocated_threshold:.1%}. "
            "Fix tagging before issuing chargeback."
        )

    return {
        "period_end": str(period_end),
        "total_cost_usd": float(total_cost),
        "unallocated_usd": float(unallocated),
        "invoices": {cc: float(v) for cc, v in invoices.items()},
    }
Common mistakes
Introducing chargeback before tagging is mature
— if significant spend is untagged when chargeback launches, the unallocated pool gets distributed in ways that cause disputes and erode trust in the entire system.
No budget pre-loading for chargeback
— teams that have not pre-allocated AI budget in their annual plan cannot absorb chargebacks. FinOps must work with Finance to load AI budgets before chargeback goes live.
Annual billing cycles for chargeback
— annual chargebacks arrive as a shock. Monthly invoices create a feedback loop that lets teams correct behaviour before accumulating large bills.
Key takeaways
Showback creates visibility without financial impact; chargeback creates financial accountability. Both require the same tagged usage ledger.
Chargeback requires >95% tag coverage, ERP integration, and a formal dispute resolution SLA before launch.
The hybrid model (showback for experimental agents, chargeback for production COGS agents) reflects different accountability needs across the agent portfolio.
Monthly chargeback cycles with 10-day dispute windows prevent the end-of-year billing shocks that damage team relationships with the FinOps function.
